name: mfi
whatItMeasures: The Money Flow Index is a volume-weighted version of RSI that measures buying and selling pressure by incorporating both price and volume data. It oscillates between 0 and 100. Often called the 'volume-weighted RSI,' it provides a more complete picture of money flowing into and out of an asset than price-only oscillators.

parameters.name: Period
parameters.rationale: The 14-period lookback mirrors the standard RSI setting and provides a balanced view of money flow over roughly three trading weeks.

howToRead: MFI below 20 indicates the asset is oversold with heavy selling volume — potential capitulation point.
MFI above 80 indicates the asset is overbought with heavy buying volume — potential exhaustion point.
MFI divergence from price (price rises but MFI falls) signals weakening buying pressure and a possible reversal.
Rising MFI with rising price confirms that the advance is supported by volume (healthy trend).
Falling MFI with falling price confirms that the decline has strong participation (likely to continue).

signalRules.buy: MFI crosses below 20 (oversold with high selling volume), especially when followed by a turn back above 20.
signalRules.sell: MFI crosses above 80 (overbought with high buying volume), especially when followed by a turn back below 80.
signalRules.neutral: MFI between 20 and 80 with no divergence or extreme reading.

limitations: MFI requires reliable volume data, which may be less meaningful for thinly traded assets or some ETFs.
Like RSI, MFI can remain at extreme levels during strong trends, generating premature reversal signals.
Volume data quality varies across exchanges and data providers, affecting MFI accuracy.
MFI is most effective in range-bound markets; in strong trends, it frequently flags false overbought/oversold conditions.
The indicator is less useful for assets with manipulated or thin volume profiles.
evidence: Backtesting on SPY by Liberatedstocktrader.com showed MFI achieving a 71% win rate while only being invested approximately 35% of the time. This means MFI-based strategies spend most of their time in cash, which significantly reduces drawdowns and market exposure risk. The trade-off is missing portions of sustained rallies, but the capital efficiency (returns relative to time invested) is notably strong.
